title: DAVID — Richard I: The Lion Who Spoke Norman French
logline: Biography of Richard I with attested crusader acclamation in Anglo-Norman register; sources on file.

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Royal Tongues — dead languages and the voices of history
brand.cta: Follow the Archive — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

sources.citation: Itinerarium Regis Ricardi et Galfredi — Third Crusade siege of Acre and crusader acclamations
sources.notes: Latin eyewitness chronicle tradition; Deus le volt formula attested in crusade literature

sources.citation: Roger of Hoveden, Chronica — Richard's captivity, ransom, and Eleanor's intercession
sources.notes: Administrative and narrative chronicle of Angevin court

sources.citation: William of Newburgh, Historia rerum anglicarum — Angevin military campaigns
sources.notes: Contemporary English historical writing in Latin

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Tonight, Richard the First — Lionheart — crusader king who spoke Anglo-Norman at court and Latin in his charters.
Born eleven fifty-seven, third son who became heir. Warrior, poet in the troubadour orbit, absentee king of England — present in every chronicle of the Third Crusade.
His world was siege engines at Acre, ransom negotiations in Germany, and a court tongue that was neither English nor Parisian French — Anglo-Norman, the language of power after Hastings.
Deus le volt! — God wills it — the crusader acclamation attested across Norman and Latin chronicles of the Third Crusade, here in Anglo-Norman pronunciation.
He died in eleven ninety-nine from a crossbow bolt at Châlus — legend larger than reign. What the corpus gives us is not his private voice, but the shouts armies recorded.

lines: PERIOD LINE: Deus le volt — attested crusade formula; Anglo-Norman delivery reconstructed from orthographic tradition
